info.title: Cloud Services for RHEL Compliance API V1
info.description: This is the API for Cloud Services for RHEL Compliance. You can find out more about Red Hat Cloud Services for RHEL at [https://console.redhat.com/](https://console.redhat.com/)

parameters.description: Query string compliant with scoped_search query language: https://github.com/wvanbergen/scoped_search/wiki/Query-language

parameters.name: sort_by
parameters.description: A string or an array of fields with an optional direction (:asc or :desc) to sort the results.

parameters.name: include
parameters.description: A comma seperated list of resources to include in the response

parameters.name: tags
parameters.description:
An array of tags to narrow down the search results. In case the value contains symbols used for separators (`/` or `=`), they need to be encoded.
e.g.: `namespace/key=value`, `insights-client/selinux-config=SELINUX%3Denforcing`

status.description: the HTTP status code applicable to this problem, expressed as a string value.

code.description: an application-specific error code, expressed as a string value.

title.description: a short, human-readable summary of the problem that SHOULD NOT change from occurrence to occurrence of the problem, except for purposes of localization.

detail.description: a human-readable explanation specific to this occurrence of the problem. Like title, this field’s value can be localized.

filter.example: name='Standard System Security Profile for Fedora'

title.example: Guide to the Secure Configuration of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7
Guide to the Secure Configuration of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7

description.example: This profile contains rules to ensure standard security baseline\nof a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 system. Regardless of your system's workload\nall of these checks should pass.

policy_type.example: Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) Essential Eight

title.example: Record Access Events to Audit Log directory

description.example:
The audit system should collect access audit log directory.
The following audit rule will assure that access to audit log directory are
collected.
-a always,exit -F dir=/var/log/audit/ -F perm=r -F auid>=1000-F auid!=unset -F key=access-audit-trail
If theis configured to use the augenrules
program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the
rule to a file with suffix .rules in the directory
/etc/audit/rules.d.
If the auditd daemon is to use the auditctl
utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the rule to
/etc/audit/audit.rules file.

rationale.example: Attempts to read the logs should be recorded, suspicious access to audit log files could be an indicator of malicious activity on a system.\nAuditing these events could serve as evidence of potential system compromise.

description.example: default - Default SELinux boolean setting. on - SELinuxboolean is enabled. off - SELinux boolean is disabled.
